shortDefinition: hs-CRP (high-sensitivity C-reactive protein) is a sensitive blood marker of systemic inflammation, used to assess cardiovascular and autoimmune risk.
fullDefinition:
## What hs-CRP measures

**hs-CRP** — **high-sensitivity C-reactive protein** — is a blood test that measures small amounts of CRP, a protein produced by the liver in response to inflammation anywhere in the body. The 'high-sensitivity' version detects levels far below the threshold of standard CRP, making it useful for assessing chronic, low-grade inflammation rather than just acute infection.

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, hs-CRP is part of evaluating cardiovascular risk, [autoimmune-disease] activity (including [hashimotos-thyroiditis]), [metabolic-syndrome], and any clinical picture where systemic inflammation may be driving symptoms.

## Why it matters

Chronic low-grade inflammation underlies many of the conditions women face in midlife — cardiovascular disease, autoimmune flares, metabolic dysfunction, depression, and cognitive decline. hs-CRP is a non-specific marker (it does not say *where* the inflammation is), but it is one of the most useful single screening tools because:

- It tracks treatment response — falling hs-CRP often signals real progress
- It identifies cardiovascular risk above and beyond cholesterol
- It can flag autoimmune and metabolic activity before more specific markers move
- It is inexpensive and widely available

hs-CRP fluctuates with acute illness, recent infection, surgery, intense exercise, and dental work. A high reading after the flu or a wisdom tooth extraction is not concerning — repeat testing 2–4 weeks later gives the more meaningful baseline.

## Reference range vs. functional/optimal range

**Conventional reference range:**
- **Low cardiovascular risk:** <1 mg/L
- **Average risk:** 1–3 mg/L
- **High risk:** >3 mg/L

Levels above 10 mg/L usually reflect an acute process (infection, injury, recent vaccination, autoimmune flare) and should be repeated after recovery.

**Functional/optimal target:** less than 1 mg/L, with many functional clinicians aiming for under 0.5 mg/L. Persistently elevated hs-CRP, even within the 'average risk' range, is worth investigating.

## What abnormal results suggest

**Elevated hs-CRP** can reflect a wide range of causes:

- Cardiovascular disease and atherosclerosis
- Autoimmune conditions (including Hashimoto's, rheumatoid arthritis, lupus)
- Insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome
- Visceral obesity
- Chronic infections (gum disease, sinus, urinary, gut)
- Sleep apnea
- Smoking, vaping, environmental exposures
- Recent acute illness (transient)

Because hs-CRP is non-specific, the value is in interpreting it alongside the rest of the picture. A persistently elevated hs-CRP in a woman with thyroid antibodies, joint pain, and fatigue points one direction; the same number in a woman with metabolic syndrome and high blood pressure points another.

**Low hs-CRP** generally reflects low systemic inflammation and is reassuring.

The encouraging news: hs-CRP is highly responsive to lifestyle and root-cause work. Improving diet quality, treating insulin resistance, addressing sleep, resolving subclinical infections, and reducing alcohol all tend to lower hs-CRP within weeks to months. Tracking hs-CRP over time is one of the most motivating ways to see whether your underlying health is improving — and a conversation worth having with your clinician.

commonQuestions.q: Should I worry about a single high hs-CRP?
commonQuestions.a: Not necessarily. Acute illness, recent surgery, dental work, intense exercise, and even a heavy training week can transiently raise hs-CRP. A single elevated reading is rarely diagnostic. Best practice is to repeat the test 2–4 weeks later under more stable conditions, with no recent illness or major exertion. If it remains elevated, the next step is to look for the underlying driver — autoimmune, metabolic, infectious, or cardiovascular — rather than treating the number itself. Persistent elevation deserves investigation; a one-off bump usually does not.

Sometimes. hs-CRP is non-specific, so it captures inflammation broadly rather than thyroid-specific autoimmunity. In Hashimoto's, antibody levels (TPO and TgAb) and clinical symptoms are usually more direct measures of disease activity. That said, hs-CRP often falls when the broader autoimmune environment improves — when gut health, gluten exposure, nutrient status, and stress are addressed. Tracking hs-CRP alongside antibodies and symptoms gives a more complete picture of how the immune system is doing overall.

The most consistently effective levers are: removing ultra-processed foods and seed oils, eating an anti-inflammatory whole-foods diet rich in omega-3s, treating insulin resistance, prioritizing 7–9 hours of sleep, treating sleep apnea if present, addressing periodontal disease, reducing alcohol, and treating underlying autoimmune or thyroid disease. Targeted nutrients — omega-3s, vitamin D, magnesium, curcumin in some cases — can support the process. Most women see meaningful drops within 8–12 weeks of consistent work, and the trend is more important than any single number.

seoTitle: hs-CRP Test: Inflammation, Heart Risk & Autoimmune Marker | MTC
seoDescription: hs-CRP measures systemic inflammation linked to cardiovascular and autoimmune risk. Learn the optimal range, what affects results, and how to lower it.
